nativeRange.habitat: Coastal lagoons, bays, reef passes, and outer reef slopes over hard substrate with rubble and sand patches, typically 5–15 m (recorded down to 80 m); adults occupy burrows in the sand and feed on current-borne zooplankton up to a few metres above the substrate

decorPreferences: open sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing
live rock with crevices for shelter
open swimming space above the substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on current-borne zooplankton hovering up to about 3 m above its burrow. In captivity generally accepts mysis shrimp, brine shrimp (vitamin-enriched), copepods, finely chopped krill, and frozen carnivore preparations. Multiple small feedings per day (commonly 3 or more) are generally advised, particularly during acclimation when individuals tend to be shy.

summary: The Blue Gudgeon Dartfish is a peaceful, reef-safe Indo-Pacific dartfish generally considered beginner-friendly. It tends to hover near the sand bed over burrows and is known to be a jumper, so a tightly sealed lid is generally advised.
careNotes:
Generally advised to keep in an established reef or fish-only-with-live-rock aquarium with a sandy substrate (commonly suggested 5+ cm deep), loose coral rubble for shelter, and open swimming space above the sand bed. This species has been reported to excavate or occupy burrows and tends to hover just above them while feeding on suspended plankton. It tends to be shy during the first days after introduction; multiple small feedings have been reported to help draw it out and encourage feeding responses.

Widely regarded as a jumper — a tight-fitting lid with no gaps is generally considered essential. Escape risk is high, consistent with most dartfish in the hobby.

Generally considered peaceful toward all tank mates, including conspecifics, and has been reported to do well kept singly, as a mated pair, or in small groups of 2–4 individuals introduced simultaneously (larger groups tend to be less seclusive). Best avoided with overtly aggressive or predatory tankmates.

Note: this species is distinct from the closely related Lyretail Blue Gudgeon Dartfish (Ptereleotris hanae), which is also sold under the same common name; the two are recorded as separate entries in this dataset.

Source spread: minimum-tank recommendations ranged from 30 US gal (~114 L, LiveAquaria, AquariumDomain) up to 50 US gal (~189 L, BlueZoo Aquatics). The value used here (150 L, ≈ 40 US gal) biases toward the larger recommendations to reflect the species' need for open swimming space and the recommendation to keep small groups. Max size sources ranged from 5 in (12.7 cm, LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain) to 5.5 in (14 cm, BlueZoo) and 14 cm (FishBase); the range used here (12–14 cm) averages across these. Temperature sources ranged from 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) at LiveAquaria, 72–80°F (22.2–26.7°C) at AquariumDomain, and 22–27°C at FishBase; the value recorded here averages across these.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been well documented in commonly available hobbyist literature. In the wild, adults have been reported to live in monogamous pairs sharing a burrow, but specific aquarium spawning protocols are not established.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection page lists Blue Gudgeon Dartfish; species-specific care details cross-referenced from LiveAquaria's species product page.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, common names (Blacktail Goby, Spot Tail Goby, Indigo Dartfish), max size (5.5 in / 14 cm), minimum tank (50 US gal / 189 L), care level (beginner), temperament (peaceful), diet (planktivore), reef-safe status, and origin.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy (Gobiiformes / Microdesmidae), max published length 14 cm TL, wild temperature 22–27°C, depth 5–80 m, and Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea and East Africa to Hawaiian/Line/Marquesas/Society Islands). Used for taxonomy, native range, and parameter cross-check.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (5 in / 12.7 cm), minimum tank (30 US gal / 114 L), water temperature (72–80°F / 22.2–26.7°C), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), reef-safe status, and grouping preference (2–4 individuals).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding protocols are not established in commonly available hobbyist sources. In the wild, pairs share burrows and are presumably substrate/burrow-spawners, but specific aquarium breeding has not been well documented.
